chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The group is exploring various models (in vitro vs. in vivo), different barriers (gut vs. blood-brain), and potential triggers (stress, ultrasound, genetics) without narrowing down to a single project.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Multiple non-speaking participants (Rosa, Faranak, Pamela) show consistent head nods, and there are occasional smiles and audible backchannels.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Connected focused ultrasound techniques to isolating blood-brain barrier permeability.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: The domain is narrowed to gut and BBB permeability, triggers, and models, but no specific research question has been articulated.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group is still in an open exploration phase, sharing knowledge from their respective fields without agreeing on a specific direction.
chunk_summary.skill_gap_description: Identified a lack of scalable in vitro models that can simultaneously capture both gut and blood-brain barrier dynamics.
chunk_summary.shared_vision_quote: we have to actually really come up with a system where you will predict triggers
chunk_summary.notable_observation: Rosa Krajmalnik-Brown actively facilitated the session by explicitly calling on participants who had not yet spoken (Pamela, Dave, Tom, Melanie), ensuring that diverse disciplinary perspectives on metabolites and barriers were integrated into the discussion.

codes.evidence: the problem is that you have if you do it through the gut brain axis, you have the intestinal barrier and then the blood brain barrier. So it's very hard to model both in an in vitro system.

codes.evidence: That's why we went for the in vivo system. So we want to see if a defect in one or the two is happening

codes.evidence: There's actually an interesting system involving focused ultrasound that you can use to temporally change the CNS barriers... that allows passage over the span of say 30 seconds and then you can close it again.
codes.explanation: Andrew introduces a novel method (focused ultrasound) to temporarily open the BBB.

codes.evidence: so that would allow you to at least isolate that barrier separate from the intestinal barrier.

codes.evidence: There's actually an interesting system

ideas_currently_on_table: Role of microbiome in enteric nervous system and epithelial barrier leakiness
Microbial triggers affecting blood-brain barrier permeability and physiology
Using focused ultrasound to temporarily isolate and study the blood-brain barrier
Investigating the gut endothelium as a distinct barrier affected by microbes
Two-hit model of barrier disruption involving genetics/microbes and stress
chunk_summaries: The session began with structured round-robin introductions where participants shared their diverse disciplinary backgrounds, ranging from neuroimmunology and the gut microbiome to chemical engineering and the enteric nervous system. Facilitator Rosa Krajmalnik-Brown synthesized the group's collective expertise and introduced the shared prompt regarding how barriers affecting the gut-brain axis are altered during disease states. The group transitioned into early divergent exploration, with Faranak and Iliyan sharing initial thoughts on the role of microbial factors in epithelial and blood-brain barrier permeability.

The group continued divergent exploration of barrier permeability, focusing on the differences between the gut and blood-brain barriers. Erin challenged the assumption that molecule size is the primary factor, noting that inflammatory mediators can cause leakiness. Andrew introduced focused ultrasound as a novel method to temporarily open the BBB, while Dave proposed investigating the gut endothelium as an understudied barrier. Rosa actively facilitated the discussion by calling on specific participants to share their expertise. The tone remained collaborative and engaged, with the group identifying several potential triggers and models but not yet converging on a specific project.
